content.text: A retrieval-augmented generation system is a pipeline of six loosely-coupled components. Each component has a single job; you can swap any one without touching the rest. Understanding what each does—and where it can fail—is the foundation for tuning and debugging a real RAG system.

attrs.alt: Indexing pipeline (Source Docs → Document Loader → Text Splitter → Embedding Model → Vector Store) and Query pipeline (User Query → embed → Vector Store → Retriever → Top-K Chunks → Generator → Answer)

content.text: The diagram shows two pipelines that share one component. The
runs offline (or on a schedule) and builds the vector store. The
runs online for every user request and reads from that store.
The loader reads raw source files and returns a list of
dict. The metadata follows the chunk through the whole pipeline and surfaces in the final answer as source attribution.
LangChain ships loaders for every common format:
each row as a Document; column names in metadata

from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader, WebBaseLoader

# PDF — one Document per page
loader = PyPDFLoader("annual_report_2025.pdf")
docs = loader.load()
print(docs[0].metadata)
# {'source': 'annual_report_2025.pdf', 'page': 0}

# Web page
loader = WebBaseLoader(
    "https://example.com/earnings",
    bs_kwargs={"parse_only": bs4.SoupStrainer("article")},  # target just <article>
)
docs = loader.load()
is worth enforcing at load time. Add fields like
here; they cost nothing to carry and enable metadata filtering at retrieval time.
A single document is too large to embed meaningfully and too long to fit many times in a context window. The splitter breaks it into overlapping chunks. Two parameters dominate the trade-off:
— target token count per chunk. Smaller chunks give higher precision (each chunk is focused); larger chunks give more context per retrieval hit.
— tokens shared between consecutive chunks. Overlap ensures a sentence split across a boundary still appears in at least one complete chunk. 10–15 % of chunk_size is typical.
, which tries to split at natural boundaries first (paragraph → sentence → word) before falling back to a hard character cut:

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,        # tokens (tiktoken or char approximation)
    chunk_overlap=64,      # carry 64 tokens from the previous chunk
    length_function=len,   # swap for tiktoken-based count for exact control
    separators=["

", "
", ".", " ", ""],  # priority list
)

chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
print(f"{len(docs)} docs → {len(chunks)} chunks")
# e.g. 12 docs → 187 chunks

Fixed-size splitting can cut a fact in half. Semantic chunking embeds each sentence, then splits wherever cosine similarity drops sharply between consecutive sentences—keeping coherent ideas together:

from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

chunker = SemanticChunker(
    OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",   # split at 95th-percentile similarity drops
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=95,
)
chunks = chunker.split_documents(docs)

Semantic chunking improves retrieval quality but adds embedding cost during indexing and is slower. Use it when document structure is inconsistent or when retrieval precision is more important than indexing speed.
start with chunk_size=512, overlap=64, RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter. Measure retrieval recall (context_recall in RAGAS) before tuning further.
The embedding model converts a text chunk into a dense vector in
. Every chunk in the index and every user query must use the same model—mixing models invalidates cosine similarity.

A bi-encoder (like a sentence-transformer) passes the text through a transformer, then mean-pools the last hidden states into a single vector. At query time the same encoder embeds the query; retrieval finds the
stored vectors with the highest dot product or cosine similarity.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(
    model="text-embedding-3-small",
    dimensions=512,      # optional: reduce to 512 to cut storage ~66 %
)

# Embed a list of texts in one batched API call
vectors = embeddings.embed_documents([chunk.page_content for chunk in chunks])
print(f"shape: {len(vectors)} × {len(vectors[0])}")
# shape: 187 × 512

# Embed a single query
q_vec = embeddings.embed_query("What was Q3 2025 revenue?")
most models have a 512–8 192 token input limit. Chunks exceeding this are silently truncated. Always verify
. With text-embedding-3-small (8 191 tokens), 512-token chunks are safe.

The vector store persists embeddings together with their source metadata and builds an approximate-nearest-neighbour (ANN) index over them. ANN trades a small accuracy loss for orders-of-magnitude speed: searching 10 million vectors with HNSW takes ~5 ms versus ~10 s for exact brute-force.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma   # or Pinecone, Weaviate, …

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Build the store from chunks — embeds automatically, stores vectors + metadata
vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(
    documents=chunks,
    embedding=embeddings,
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",   # local disk; omit for in-memory
)
fastest in-process ANN; no metadata filter
For production at scale: Pinecone, Weaviate, or Milvus. For a prototype or self-hosted small corpus: Chroma or FAISS. If you already run Postgres: pgvector with HNSW index.
The retriever translates an embedded query into a ranked list of the most-relevant chunks from the vector store. LangChain exposes a uniform interface:

# Basic similarity search (cosine / dot-product ANN)
retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_type="similarity",
    search_kwargs={"k": 6},   # return top-6 chunks
)
results = retriever.invoke("What was Q3 2025 revenue?")
for doc in results:
    print(doc.metadata["source"], "—", doc.page_content[:120])

Plain similarity search can return six near-identical chunks. MMR alternates between relevance and diversity: each additional result must be similar to the query but dissimilar to already-selected results.
dict alongside the ANN query. Only chunks matching the filter are scored, which is faster than post-filtering:
The generator reads the retrieved chunks—injected into a structured prompt—and produces a grounded answer. The key principle: the model should act as a reader, not as a knowledge store.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)  # temp=0 for factual grounding

GROUNDING_PROMPT = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("""
You are a precise assistant. Answer the question using ONLY the context below.
If the answer is not in the context, say "I don't have that information."
Cite the source document for every claim you make using [source: <filename>].

Context:
{context}

Question: {question}
""")

def format_docs(docs):
    return "

".join(
        f"[source: {d.metadata.get('source', 'unknown')}]
{d.page_content}"
        for d in docs
    )

rag_chain = (
    {"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
    | GROUNDING_PROMPT
    | llm
    | StrOutputParser()
)

answer = rag_chain.invoke("What was Q3 2025 revenue?")
print(answer)
# "Q3 2025 revenue was $2.1B [source: acme-10q-q3-2025.pdf]."

is recommended for factual RAG. A higher temperature lets the model deviate from retrieved context, increasing hallucination risk. For summarization or creative Q&A over documents, 0.1–0.3 may be acceptable.
The two pipelines fit together in a single script. In production you run the indexing pipeline once (or on a schedule) and the query pipeline on every user request.

# ── INDEXING PIPELINE ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

# 1. Load
loader = PyPDFLoader("acme-10q-q3-2025.pdf")
docs = loader.load()

# 2. Split
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64)
chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)

# 3 + 4. Embed and store
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(chunks, embeddings, persist_directory="./db")

# ── QUERY PIPELINE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough

# 5. Retriever
retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 6})

# 6. Generator
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Answer using ONLY the context. Cite sources.

"
    "Context:
{context}

Question: {question}"
)

def fmt(docs):
    return "

".join(f"[{d.metadata['source']}]
{d.page_content}" for d in docs)

chain = (
    {"context": retriever | fmt, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
    | prompt
    | llm
    | StrOutputParser()
)

print(chain.invoke("What drove revenue growth in Q3 2025?"))
For a chat UI, streaming the generator token-by-token reduces perceived latency. LangChain LCEL chains support

import asyncio

async def stream_rag(question: str):
    """Stream the RAG answer token-by-token."""
    # Retrieve synchronously first (ANN is fast enough)
    context_docs = retriever.invoke(question)
    context = fmt(context_docs)

    # Then stream the generator
    print(f"Sources: {[d.metadata['source'] for d in context_docs]}")
    print("Answer: ", end="", flush=True)
    async for token in chain.astream(question):
        print(token, end="", flush=True)
    print()  # newline after stream ends

asyncio.run(stream_rag("What drove Q3 2025 revenue growth?"))
If your app framework is synchronous (Flask, Django), use
. Both yield string tokens; wrap in an HTTP
response to forward them to a browser.
The clean interface between components means you can swap any one without changing the others—as long as both sides speak the same type (
replace PyPDFLoader with WebBaseLoader—same Document output, rest of pipeline unchanged.
replace RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with SemanticChunker—same Document list, rest unchanged.
replace text-embedding-3-small with bge-large (self-hosted)—must re-index, but query pipeline code doesn't change.
replace Chroma with Pinecone—update one constructor call.
replace gpt-4o with claude-sonnet or llama-3—one ChatOpenAI import change.
), not the concrete class. That keeps every component independently testable and swappable.
Use the same model class and version for indexing and querying
Verify chunk_size ≤ model max tokens; use tiktoken to count exactly
Add source, page, doc_type in the loader's metadata dict
Set temperature=0 for factual grounding; only raise for creative tasks
Increase k (6–10 is common); measure context_recall with RAGAS
Forgetting to re-index after swapping embeddings
Always wipe and rebuild the vector store when the embedding model changes
A RAG system has six components: Loader, Splitter, Embedding Model, Vector Store, Retriever, Generator.
Every component has a single responsibility and can be swapped independently.
Metadata attached at load time propagates through the pipeline and enables filtering and citations.
chunk_size=512, overlap=64 is a safe starting point; tune after measuring retrieval recall.
The embedding model must be identical for indexing and querying—mismatches silently destroy retrieval quality.
temperature=0 in the generator anchors answers to retrieved context and reduces hallucination.
